title: Plexus as Runtime: Single-tool MCP via Tactus code execution
description:
# Plexus as Runtime — Single-tool MCP via Tactus code execution

## Thesis

Replace the current ~64-tool Plexus MCP server surface with a single `execute_tactus` MCP tool that exposes Plexus functionality as a programmable Tactus Lua API.

> Plexus becomes a runtime platform for agentic code execution rather than a machine with a bunch of tools sticking out of it.

## Why

The current 64-tool MCP catalog burns context budget before any real work begins (industry reports show 50k–150k tokens of tool definitions are common in tool-rich MCP setups; Anthropic measured 98.7% reductions on real workflows by switching to code execution). More importantly, a flat tool catalog cannot express:

- Sub-agent composition (Tactus is fundamentally an agent-orchestration DSL — each Worker is its own LLM with its own context window, tools, and model choice).
- The full Plexus ML ladder (regex → classical ML → embeddings → fine-tuned BERT → small LLM → big LLM → multi-agent) as cost-aware first-class primitives.
- Train-evaluate-deploy loops as single coherent programs.
- Versioned, savable, reusable agent workflows that close the loop back into Procedures.

## Scope (everything is v1, no v2-deferred bucket)

All ~56 substantive Plexus operations are exposed in the v1 Lua API surface, including the dispatched/long-running ones (evaluations, reports, procedures). HITL-by-default for ~14 destructive operations. Distributed budget infrastructure is mandatory v1, not deferred — running evaluations is core to Plexus.

## Architectural commitments

1. **One MCP tool** (`execute_tactus`) with a small boot prompt (≤5k tokens hard ceiling) and on-demand docs via `plexus.docs.*`.
2. **Tactus host-module mechanism (Tier 1)** — minimal upstream Tactus change to allow `local plexus = require('plexus')` from sandboxed Lua.
3. **PlexusModule facade** — ~14 namespaces (`plexus.scorecards.*`, `plexus.score.*`, `plexus.evaluation.*`, `plexus.feedback.*`, `plexus.predict`, `plexus.dataset.*`, `plexus.docs.*`, `plexus.budget.*`, `plexus.cost.*`, etc.) — sharing service implementations with the existing MCP tools (no duplicate logic).
4. **Hierarchical, divisible, ambient budgets** with categories (usd, wallclock, tokens, llm_calls, tool_calls, depth, workers). Distributed propagation through the existing task-dispatch system.
5. **HITL-by-default for destructive operations** — auto-fired `Human.approve` before mutations; `no_confirm = true` escape hatch with audit logging.
6. **Streaming + handle ergonomics** for long-running operations (wire trace bridge to MCP streaming; handle-based async for very long ops).
7. **Dual-stack migration** — old 64 tools coexist; new tool is opt-in; sunset criteria written down.

## Key safety properties

- Conservative default budget (~`$0.25 / 60s / depth 3`) so a forgetful caller cannot accidentally burn money.
- Account-level caps independent of per-call budgets.
- Recursion depth tracked across dispatch boundaries.
- Tracing recorded for every `execute_tactus` call (source, calls, costs, errors) for replay/debug.
- Module bindings carry per-invocation context immutably (no cross-tenant leak via captured references).

## Out of scope (separate initiatives, noted as comments below)

- Tactus package system Tier 2 (full third-party package system with manifests, versioning, distribution, capabilities)
- Existing MCP server consolidation (drift between `MCP/server.py` / `MCP/asgi_app.py` / `MCP/plexus_fastmcp_server.py`, dead code, dual `get_plexus_documentation`)
- Cost-aware promotion gates (cost-per-decision metric, volume projection at promotion, per-score daily ceilings, anomaly detection — the "scaling-aware design" track)

## Proceed criterion

Epic 1 (empirical validation spike) is gating. ≥85% first-try success rate from frontier LLMs writing real Plexus-flavored Tactus Lua against a stub PlexusModule + 2k-token boot prompt + on-demand docs is required to commit to the rest of the initiative.

## Rough scope estimate

5–7 weeks of focused work for the full v1, dominated by the distributed-budget infrastructure (Epic 4). The Tactus host-module work (Epic 2) is small (~50 lines + tests, ~1–2 days). Service-layer refactor (within Epic 3) for ~15 of the existing tools is the second-largest cost.

comments.author: ryan
comments.text:
# Out-of-scope items (track separately when ready)

These came up during the planning conversation but are explicitly OUT of scope for this initiative. They warrant their own initiatives or epics when the team is ready to take them on. Recorded here for conversational continuity.

## Tactus package system Tier 2

A real third-party package system for Tactus: manifests, semver versioning, dependency graphs, file-system discovery (e.g., `tactus_modules/`), capability declarations, central registry, `tactus install` CLI, polyglot package format. Plexus is the proving-ground first user via Tier 1 (Epic 2) but Tier 2 is its own Tactus-platform investment with substantial design surface.

Forward compatibility: the Tier 1 `register_python_module` API is a strict subset of what Tier 2 would offer. Tier 1 code keeps working when Tier 2 lands.

## Existing MCP server consolidation

Resolve drift between `MCP/server.py`, `MCP/asgi_app.py`, and `MCP/plexus_fastmcp_server.py` (each registers a different subset of tools). Remove dead `MCP/tools/score/management.py`. Consolidate the dual `get_plexus_documentation` implementations (`MCP/tools/documentation/docs.py` vs `MCP/tools/util/docs.py`). Establish a single source of truth for the existing MCP surface.

This is a meaningful housekeeping epic. Held separate from this initiative so neither blocks the other.

## Cost-aware promotion gates

The "scaling-aware design" track that's distinct from runtime budgets:
- Cost-per-decision as a first-class evaluation metric (alongside AC1, accuracy, recall).
- Volume projection at champion-promotion time ("estimated monthly cost change: +$X").
- Per-score and per-scorecard daily cost ceilings (independent of per-call budgets).
- Cost-trend anomaly detection on time-series data.
- Pre-deployment cost simulation (replay against cached items, project cost, flag if cost-per-decision regressed >20%).

Plexus already has the data via `CostAccumulator` and `plexus_cost_analysis`; what's missing is the surface that uses the data for design-time cost awareness.

This is the cure for the "successful but expensive at scale" failure mode (different from runtime cost runaways, which Epic 4 handles). Worth a separate initiative on the Plexus-platform side.

## Conversational record

This plan emerged from a long collaborative design conversation. Key inflection points:

1. Initial framing: "collapse 64 MCP tools into one execute_tactus tool" for context savings.
2. Reframe: "tool calls are sub-agent calls" — Tactus is fundamentally an agent-orchestration DSL.
3. Reframe: "tool calls can leverage ML models" — Plexus's full ML stack (regex → classical ML → embeddings → BERT → small LLM → big LLM → multi-agent) becomes a cost-aware ladder of primitives.
4. Reframe: "Plexus becomes a runtime platform for agentic code execution rather than a machine with tools sticking out of it."
5. Reframe: third-party module support in Tactus (Plexus is NOT in stdlib; it's a host-registered module).
6. User correction: evaluations cannot be deferred to v2. Distributed budget infrastructure becomes mandatory v1 work.

The Munger-style inversion exercise ("how could we screw this up?") produced the safety properties and design rules baked into the epics: hard token ceiling, conservative default budget, HITL-by-default for destructive ops, single accounting choke point, mandatory tracing, per-invocation context binding, account-level caps independent of per-call budgets, 80%/100% kill semantics.

comments.author: ryan
comments.text:
# Initiative structure index

## Epics under this initiative

| Epic | ID | Priority | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | plx-f978d2 | 0 | Empirical validation spike (GATING) |
| 2 | plx-cb988c | 1 | Tactus host-module mechanism (Tier 1) |
| 3 | plx-7c2c6b | 1 | PlexusModule facade and service-layer refactor |
| 4 | plx-a4b033 | 0 | Distributed budget infrastructure |
| 5 | plx-7895cc | 1 | HITL-by-default for destructive operations |
| 6 | plx-07dc0d | 1 | execute_tactus MCP tool |
| 7 | plx-247588 | 1 | Streaming and handle ergonomics |
| 8 | plx-9ffd49 | 1 | plexus.docs content and execute_tactus boot prompt |
| 9 | plx-b953e9 | 1 | End-to-end test infrastructure |
| 10 | plx-986bdf | 2 | Migration and dual-stack rollout |

## Stories filed under Epic 1 (the gating spike)

- plx-15260b — Frontier models write correct Lua against PlexusModule stub
- plx-aed563 — Streaming progress for long-running operations remains usable
- plx-601f15 — Boot prompt and discovery overhead stay within 5k tokens

## Tasks filed under Epic 1

- plx-7041b9 — Curate the 10–15 representative spike tasks
- plx-f44454 — Build stub PlexusModule for the spike (read-only operations)
- plx-a35142 — Stub long-running plexus.evaluation.run with simulated streaming and budget
- plx-8c6855 — Write initial boot prompt for execute_tactus (≤2k tokens)
- plx-952377 — Build the spike test harness
- plx-724038 — Run the spike across 4 frontier models and capture results
- plx-8e009e — Produce go/no-go report from spike results

## Chores filed under other epics

- plx-39099d — Plexus procedure docs refresh (under Epic 8) — prerequisite for boot prompt content

## Ordering / dependencies

- Epic 1 (spike) is GATING. Result determines whether to proceed.
- Epic 2 (Tactus host-module) can begin in parallel with Epic 1 (small upstream work).
- Epic 4 (budget infra) is the dominant cost. Includes a sub-task to first explore the existing task-dispatch system before designing.
- Epic 8's chore (procedure docs refresh, plx-39099d) is a prerequisite for the boot prompt work in Epic 8 itself.
- Epics 3, 5, 6, 7, 9, 10 all depend on Epic 2's API existing.

Stories and tasks for Epics 2–10 will be filed as those epics approach implementation, per the "use minimum commands" discipline in CONTRIBUTING_AGENT.md.
